output:
# Root causes: INSERT_LARGE_DATA, LOCK_CONTENTION

## Diagnosis
1. INSERT_LARGE_DATA
2. LOCK_CONTENTION

## Reasoning
| Suspect | Strength | Evidence |
|---|---:|---|
| INSERT_LARGE_DATA | Strong | Dominant statement was 41 calls of `INSERT INTO table1 SELECT generate_series(...)`, producing 820,000 rows, 270,453 ms total time, 6,596 ms mean time, and 569 MB WAL; database `tup_inserted=820,841`. |
| LOCK_CONTENTION | Strong | The workload used about 100 concurrent writers on one hot scratch table (`table1`, 20 `varchar(100)` columns, 20,000 rows, `commit_interval=1`), forcing serialization on tuple/relation-extension locks even though post-run waits were transient and now show 0 waiters. |
| VACUUM | Absent | All surviving user tables show `n_dead_tup=0`, `n_tup_del=0`, `vacuum_count=0`, `autovacuum_count=0`; no VACUUM statement or progress exists. |
| REDUNDANT_INDEX | Absent | Only legitimate PK and UNIQUE(email) indexes exist; 0 duplicate/overlapping indexes and no `CREATE INDEX` on `table1`. |
| FETCH_LARGE_DATA | Absent | No `orders`/`lineitem` tables or mass SELECT workload; largest SELECT returned 119 rows, and 820k rows came from an INSERT...SELECT write path. |

INSERT_LARGE_DATA is a root cause because the measured write volume itself created sustained load: 820k inserted rows, 270.453 seconds of statement time, and 569 MB of WAL. That is not just a symptom of contention; it is the volume facet of the anomaly.

LOCK_CONTENTION is also a root cause because the same workload shape put many concurrent writers against the same hot table with per-transaction commits. It is not merely downstream of insert volume: the concurrency facet independently amplifies latency through transient lock serialization that disappears in the quiescent post-run snapshot.

## Ruled Out
- VACUUM: ruled out by `n_dead_tup=0`, `n_tup_del=0`, `vacuum_count=0`, `autovacuum_count=0`, no VACUUM statements, and empty `pg_stat_progress_vacuum`.
- REDUNDANT_INDEX: ruled out by only 7 legitimate indexes (PKs plus UNIQUE(email)), 0 duplicate indexes, and no `CREATE INDEX` on `table1`.
- FETCH_LARGE_DATA: ruled out by absence of `orders`/`lineitem` and mass SELECTs; largest SELECT returned 119 rows, while the 820,000 rows were produced by an INSERT...SELECT write.

## How the anomaly manifested
The benchmark created scratch table `table1` with 20 wide varchar columns and used a high-concurrency write workload against it. The dominant live metric was repeated bulk `INSERT INTO table1 SELECT generate_series(...)`, totaling 41 calls and 820,000 inserted rows. That generated 569 MB of WAL and 270,453 ms of cumulative statement time. Because roughly 100 concurrent writers targeted the same table with `commit_interval=1`, the run also suffered transient hot-table lock serialization that was gone by the time post-run locks were sampled.

## Confidence
High — both selected causes are directly supported by workload shape and live metrics, while the other three candidates have explicit negative evidence.

FINAL ANSWER: INSERT_LARGE_DATA, LOCK_CONTENTION
